rulings.Case Title: C.I. ENERGIA SOLAR S.A., INC., VS. BAGATELOS GLASS SYSTEMS, INC., ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Undertaking From Out Of State And Foreign Corporation Ci Energia Solar Sa
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, February 25, 2019, 2018, Line 2, DEFENDANT BAGATELOS GLASS SYSTEMS, INC.,, THE HANOVER INSURANCE COMPANY's Motion For Undertaking From Out Of State And Foreign Corporation Ci Energia Solar Sa. Defendant Bagatelos Glass Systems, Inc.'s motion for undertaking is denied. Bagatelos fails to present competent evidence demonstrating that there is a "reasonable possibility" that it will prevail within the meaning of Code of Civil Procedure § 1030(a). The only evidence that Bagatelos relies upon is the declaration of counsel Christopher Rodriguez. The Court sustains C.I. Energia's objections to that declaration. The declaration fails to show that Mr. Rodriguez has personal knowledge regarding Exhibits C through F. In addition, there is no analysis as to how the attached documents show that C.I. Energia provided a defective product such that Bagatelos will prevail. For example, Bagatelos argues that Ex. D demonstrates that the windows failed to comply with the product specifications. (Mot., 8:13-21.) Ex. D is a project manual that is 1545 pages long. Bagaletos does not highlight any particular portion of that document and did not even provide a copy of it to the Court. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for C.I. Energia is required to prepare a proposed order which states that the motion was not opposed and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: BRETT TODOROFF VS. JOE QUINN BRETZ
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Strike Portions Of Plaintiffs First Amended Complaint By Defendant Joe Quinn Bretz
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, February 25, 2019, 2018, Line 3, DEFENDANT JOE BRETZ, RANDOLPH BRETZ's Motion To Strike Portions Of Plaintiffs First Amended Complaint By Defendant Joe Quinn Bretz. Defendants Joe Quinn Bretz and Randolph Bretz's motion to strike portions of Plaintiffs Brett Todoroff and Mark Finley's first amended complaint is denied. The allegations Defendants seek to strike form a basis for the first cause of action for dissolution of the limited liability company, to which Defendants have not demurred, and the second cause of action for breach of contract, which the Court previously ruled is sufficiently pled. Plaintiffs have not "admitted" that the sole contract at issue in this action is the Subscription Agreement. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Plaintiffs is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: BRETT TODOROFF VS. JOE QUINN BRETZ
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, February 25, 2019, 2018, Line 3, DEFENDANT JOE BRETZ, RANDOLPH BRETZ's DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT.(part 1 of 2) Defendants Joe Quinn Bretz and Randolph Bretz's demurrer to Plaintiffs Brett Todoroff and Mark Finley's first amended complaint is overruled as to the second cause of action for breach of contract and the fifth cause of action for declaratory relief, and is sustained as to the third cause of action for fraud with 30 days' leave to amend. The Court takes judicial notice of the Subscription Agreement, which both parties discuss. The Court previously denied Defendants' motion for judgment on the pleadings as to the breach of contract cause of action. Defendants' newly-asserted contention that the contract is illegal and unenforceable is unavailing. First, Defendants have not established that the contract lacks any lawful object. The First Amended Complaint alleges in paragraphs 14 and 21 that the primary purpose of the agreement was to acquire ownership in specified real properties and that the limited liability company was formed for the purpose of specific land and agricultural development. The Subscription Agreement and related documents do not establish on their face that the agreement lacked any lawful object. Second, while cultivation and sale of cannabis is illegal under federal law and contracts that violate federal law can be void and unenforceable under California law (Kashani v. Tsann Kuen China Enter. Co. (2004) 118 Cal.App.4th 531, 543), the enforceability of an allegedly illegal contract is dependent on the particular facts and circumstances. (See South Tahoe Gas Co. v. Hofmann Land Improvement Co. (1972) 25 Cal.App.3d 750, 759 ["In each case, the extent of enforceability and the kind of remedy granted depend upon a variety of factors, including the policy of the transgressed law, the kind of illegality and the particular facts."].) California has legalized the cultivation and sale of cannabis under regulated circumstances that could warrant enforcement of the agreements. (See Bus. & Prof. Code § 26000 et seq., the Medicinal and Adult-Use Cannabis Regulation and Safety Act.) Third, Defendants cannot equitably avoid enforcement of agreements that they themselves allegedly initiated. The second cause of action for breach of contract is otherwise properly alleged against Defendant Joe Quinn Bretz in his individual capacity because the first amended complaint alleges an alter ego theory and Joe Quinn Bretz's name appears on the Subscription Agreement as a Managing Member. The third cause of action for fraud must be alleged with greater particularity. The amendment to the First Amended Complaint again fails to specify (1) the dates on which Defendants made the alleged oral and written misrepresentations; (2) which specific misrepresentations were made in writing; and (3) which of the two Defendants made each of the alleged misleading statements. Further, to the extent that Plaintiffs allege that Defendants falsely promised that their investment "would result in a substantial return on investment" and that "their investment was safe and secure," Plaintiffs must allege with particularity why their purported reliance on such alleged representations was justifiable in light of Plaintiffs' written acknowledgement in the Subscription Agreement that their investment "represents a speculative investment involving a high degree of risk" and could result in "a complete loss of such investment." The Court previously denied Defendants' motion for judgment on the pleadings as to the declaratory relief cause of action, which pleads a present controversy between the parties. Plaintiffs should incorporate all amendments to the complaint into a single amended complaint, rather than filing a further amendment to the First Amended Complaint. (end of part 1 of 2) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: JASMINA ZALIHIC VS. LYFT, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Lyft Inc.'S Motion To Compel Plaintiff'S Further Deposition
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, February 25, 2019, Line 3, Defendant LYFT Inc.'s Motion To Compel Plaintiff's Further Deposition Pro Tem Judge Greg Groeneveld, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Counsel to appear. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to greg@greggroeneveldlaw.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: TROY KING VS. BOYS AND GIRLS CLUBS OF SAN FRANCISCO, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, February 25, 2019, 2018, Line 7, DEFENDANT BOYS & GIRLS CLUBS OF SAN FRANCISCO's DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT. Defendant Boys & Girls Clubs of San Francisco's demurrer to Plaintiff Troy King's second amended complaint is sustained as to the sole cause of action for intentional infliction of emotional distress without leave to amend. Mr. King's intentional infliction of emotional distress claim is time-barred. Claims for intentional infliction of emotional distress have a two-year statute of limitations. (Code Civ. Proc. § 335.1; Wassmann v. South Orange Cty. Comm. College Dist. (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 825, 852-53.) "A cause of action for intentional infliction of emotional distress accrues, and the statute of limitations begins to run, once the plaintiff suffers severe emotional distress as a result of outrageous conduct on the part of the defendant." (Wassmann, 24 Cal.App.5th at 853.) Liberally construing the second amended complaint, Mr. King claims to have suffered emotional distress as a result of learning in 2012 that Club employee Nate Ford had full knowledge that a coworker, rather than Mr. King, had been guilty of sexual involvement with a minor of which Mr. King had been accused in 2001, and that Mr. Ford failed to report the information to absolve Mr. King from blame and restore his reputation. Mr. King did not file his original complaint in this action until May 21, 2018, more than two years after 2012, so his cause of action is barred. It is appropriate to sustain a demurrer without leave to amend where the claim is time-barred. (Yee v. Mobile Home Park Rental Review Bd. (1998) 62 Cal.App.4th 1409, 1429.) Mr. King's citation to Juarez v. Boy Scouts of America, Inc. (2000) 81 Cal.App.4th 377 is inapposite as that case involves a heightened duty of care of businesses to children, not to an employee or former employee such as Mr. King. Moreover, the minor's 2017 text messages carry no weight here. Even if they were referenced in the second amended complaint-and they are not-these messages cannot form the basis for Defendant's liability as she is alleged to be a former client of Defendant, not its employee or agent. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: STEAMWORKS MANAGEMENT, LLC VS. GREAT WORKS, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Clarification Of October 10, 2018 Order Granting Motion (1) To Compel Appearances At Depositions, (2) To Compel Production Of Documents At Deposition, And (3) For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, February 25, 2019, Line 2, PLAINTIFF ROSS MOORE's Motion For Clarification Of October 10, 2018 Order Granting Motion (1) To Compel Appearances At Depositions, (2) To Compel Production Of Documents At Deposition, And (3) For Sanctions Pro Tem Judge James Fleming, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The Court consolidates all motions for relief referable to its Order of October 10, 2018 and not otherwise disposed of. Pursuant to the authority of Le Francois v. Goel, 35 Cal. 4th 1094 (2005), said Order is reconsidered and partially revised as follows: The Motion to Compel Depositions of Great Works Custodian of Records, Earl G. Stokes and Laurence Hickey is granted. Said depositions shall be scheduled within thirty days of the date of this Order unless otherwise stipulated in writing. Deponent Custodian of Records of Great Works, Inc. may assert a claim of attorney-client or attorney work product privilege as to documents noticed to be produced at deposition on condition all such documents are identified in a detailed privilege log produced no later than ten days prior to the date of commencement of deposition questioning. The privilege log shall include the name of document author(s), recipient(s), date(s) of writing and transmission, category number(s) to which responsive and subject matter(s) of the writing sufficient to identify the topic(s) addressed. The same procedure shall be followed if deponents Stokes and/or Hickey are noticed to produce documents at their depositions. The Court retains jurisdiction to determine whether privileges asserted apply to the documents in question upon a properly noticed motion. All documents as to which no claim of privilege is asserted shall be produced five days prior to the date of deposition questioning. Counsel to moving party shall submit an appropriate order via email in Word format. The parties are directed to follow the usual procedure to request or waive oral argument and to give notice thereof. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to jameshfleming@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: MADISON S. PRINCE-JUDD ET AL VS. ZHAOHUA FENG ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Strike Punitive Damages From The First Amended Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, February 25, 2019, 2018, Line 12, DEFENDANT ZHAOHUA FENG's Motion To Strike Punitive Damages From The First Amended Complaint. Defendant Zhaohua Feng's motion to strike those portions of the first amended complaint that seek punitive damages, including the "Punitive Damages Allegations" contained in paragraphs 15 and 16, is granted. (See Turman v. Turning Point of Central California, Inc. (2010) 191 Cal.App.4th 53, 63 [affirming order striking punitive damages allegations].) In order to state a prima facie claim for punitive damages, a complaint must set forth the elements stated in the general punitive damage statute, Civil Code section 3294, including allegations that the defendant has been guilty of oppression, fraud, or malice. (Civ. Code § 3294(a).) "Malice" is defined as conduct "intended by the defendant to cause injury to plaintiff, or despicable conduct that is carried on by the defendant with a willful and conscious disregard for the rights or safety of others." (Civ. Code § 3294(c)(1).) Absent an intent to injure the plaintiff, malice requires more than a "willful and conscious" disregard of the plaintiff's interests; the additional component of "despicable conduct" must be found. (College Hospital, Inc. v. Superior Court (1994) 8 Cal.4th 704, 725.) Punitive damages are appropriate if the defendant's actions are reprehensible, fraudulent, or in blatant violation of law or policy. The mere carelessness or ignorance of the defendant does not justify the imposition of punitive damages. Punitive damages are proper only when the tortious conduct rises to levels of extreme indifference to the plaintiff's rights, a level which decent citizens should not have to tolerate. (Pacific Gas and Electric Company v. Superior Court (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1150, 1170-76.) The allegations in the first amended complaint that defendant drove a motor vehicle illegally using his cell phone and speeding through a busy street with heavy traffic, and that distracted driving is looked down upon and considered a dangerous and unsafe practice by many people, may amount to recklessness but do not rise to the level of despicable conduct carried on by the defendant with a willful and conscious disregard for the rights or safety of others required by Civ. Code § 3294(c)(1). (See Lackner v. North (2006) 135 Cal.App.4th 1188, 1209-10 [evidence that snowboarder who struck skier was not "despicable" so as to support punitive damages claim; even if snowboarder was reckless in traveling at high speed in rest area without looking in the direction he was heading, he did not hit skier intentionally and attempted to avoid her].) Taylor v. Superior Court (1979) 24 Cal.3d 890, relied upon by plaintiff, is distinguishable both because it was decided before the statute was amended in 1987 to require a showing of despicable conduct and on its facts. (Id. at 1212 & n.14.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: DAVID GOLDMAN ET AL VS. CALIFORNIA HIGHWAY PATROL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion For A Preliminary Injunction
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, February 25, 2019, 2018, Line 13, PLAINTIFF DAVID GOLDMAN, WILD RIVERS TRANSPORT LLC, JOSEPH LOPEZ's Motion For A Preliminary Injunction. (part 1 of 2) Plaintiffs David Goldman, Wild Rivers Transport LLC, and Joseph Lopez's motion for preliminary injunction is denied. Plaintiffs have failed to show either a reasonable probability of success on the merits or an imminent threat of irreparable harm. (San Francisco Newspaper Printing Co. v. Superior Court (1985) 170 Cal.App.3d 438, 442 [holding that an injunction must not issue unless it is reasonably probable that the moving party will prevail on the merits]; Tahoe Keys Property Owners' Assn. v. State Water Resources Control Bd. (1994) 23 Cal.App.4th 1459, 1471 [holding that issuing a preliminary injunction against a public agency requires a "significant showing of irreparable injury"].) The U.S. Department of Justice's Guide to Equitable Sharing for State and Local Law Enforcement Agencies illustrates how states can cooperate with the federal government in seizing cannabis assets, but does not establish that Defendant California Highway Patrol (CHP) has a policy of targeting licensed cannabis businesses in California for purposes of forfeiture. Furthermore, references in the David Goldman and Joseph Lopez Declarations to media accounts of CHP seizures are vague, constitute inadmissible hearsay, and do not evince the existence of a broader CHP policy. The Richard Peter Barry Declaration presents only a single account of seizure by CHP and the circumstances of that incident as related by Barry are disputed by CHP. Plaintiffs did not provide evidence that Wild Rivers Transport was, in fact, acting in accordance with its license and that the seized funds were lawful proceeds of licensed cannabis business. The reference to CHP's website in Plaintiffs' reply brief indicates only that CHP engages in forfeiture of assets to federal agencies where the proceeds are derived from illegal activities. This is not enough to establish the existence of a CHP policy of seizure and forfeiture of legally obtained cannabis proceeds. For these reasons, there is insufficient evidence to conclude that Plaintiffs have a reasonable probability of success on the merits in showing the existence of a CHP policy that runs afoul of Health and Safety Code section 11471.2. Similarly, Plaintiffs cannot demonstrate that they will be irreparably harmed by an alleged CHP policy because two of the declarants (Goldman and Lopez) have had no encounter with CHP. Any harm to them through decreased access to legal medical and recreational marijuana is too tenuous to be considered imminent or irreparable. (See East Bay Mun. Utility Dist. v. Dept. of Forestry & Fire Protection (1996) 43 Cal.App.4th 1113, 1126 [holding that plaintiffs must present actual evidence of harm that is "immediately likely" to occur]; Tahoe Keys Property Owners' Assn., 23 Cal.App.4th at 1471 [holding that plaintiff must make a "significant showing of irreparable injury"].) The Barry Declaration does not establish impending harm because it only addresses a single incident, which is insufficient to establish the existence of a CHP policy of targeting the legal cannabis industry for forfeiture to the federal government. Without a reasonable likelihood of recurrence, there is insufficient risk of irreparable injury to issue a preliminary injunction. (See East Bay Mun. Utility Dist., 43 Cal.App.4th at 1126 ["An injunction should not issue as a remedy for past acts which are not likely to recur."].)(end of part 1 of 2)=(302/EPS)
